title: The Times Loop ⏱️
description: The simplest way to run code a specific number of times.

slides.title: The Simplest Loop ⏱️
slides.content:
# The `times` Loop

What if you don't need to evaluate conditions, but just want to execute a block of code exactly 5 times?

Because everything in Ruby is an object (including Numbers!), you can literally just call the `.times` method on any integer.

quizData.question: What keyword is placed immediately after the `.times` method to signify the passing of a block of code?
quizData.explanation: You use `do` and `end` to define a chunk of code (a block) that the `.times` method should execute.

slides.title: Tracking the Count 🔢
slides.content:
If you want to know exactly which loop iteration you are currently on, the `.times` method can hand you an index variable.

You pass it a variable name wrapped in "pipes" `| |` right after the `do` keyword.

`5.times do |index|`

slides.title: Piping in Variables
slides.content: Run this code. **Notice deeply:** The `index` variable automatically starts counting at `0`, not `1`!
slides.codeSnippet:
5.times do |index|
  puts "This is loop number #{index}"
end

quizData.question: What symbol wraps around the block parameter (like `|index|`) which captures the current loop count?
quizData.explanation: Ruby uses the 'pipe' or 'bar' character `|` to pass variables into blocks.

slides.title: Zero-Indexed 🏁
slides.content:
Did you notice the counting started at zero?

Programming languages are **0-indexed**. That means when the computer counts things, it always begins with `0`.

So `5.times` counts like this: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4.
